denial_pattern_sources: peer_to_peer_window_verbal_only: AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Physician Survey; documented in multiple state PA reform laws (TX HB 2002, VA SB 1272)
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter confirms the outcome of our review of the request for Pelvic floor physical therapy (12 visits) related to Pelvic floor dysfunction postpartum (N81.9).

After clinical review using UnitedHealthcare Medical Policies and Optum clinical criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Home exercise booklet is sufficient. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for prior authorization determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer demands a cheaper modality (HSAT vs in-lab sleep study; oral vs infusion).

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Pelvic floor physical therapy (12 visits). If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
clinical_context:
The 34-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Pelvic floor dysfunction postpartum (N81.9). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Pelvic floor physical therapy (12 visits) as the next appropriate step.

Prior vaginal delivery with levator tear on ultrasound. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on modality substitution without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
